text: Hydrogen fuel cells are considered to be one of the major alternatives for vehicle power supply in the future. Their widespread application is, however, restricted due to the slow oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) kinetics at the cathode. This necessitates not only highly active but also long-lasting and economical catalysts. Based on metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), we designed and synthesized a non-precious dual-metal single-atom catalyst (SAC) with different Co-N₄ and Zn-N₄ centers. The as-obtained Zn/Co-N-C SAC showed a high ORR performance of E1/2 at 0.938 V vs RHE along with good stability with a minor cathodic shift of ΔE1/2 = -8.5 mV after 50,000 cycles. The resulting catalyst showed even better performance in an alkaline fuel cell with a peak power density of ~1 W cm⁻², which is a record value for single-atom catalysts and the highest reported value for a SAC-based AFC. Through *operando* X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) studies on the catalytic behavior, it was confirmed that the Co-N₄ centers in the Zn/Co-N-C SAC were responsible for the ORR activity. Furthermore, the axial oxygenated species is expected to be adsorbed at the Co centers to form the catalytically active Oads-Co-N4 species. The potential-dependent microkinetic modeling (MKG) and core-level shift calculations give further insight into the catalytic activity of the Co-N₄ centers in the Zn/Co-N-C SAC. Therefore, the present study gives a clear and straightforward explanation of the mechanism of the single-atom catalysts of the dual-metal type and acts as an example for designing new catalysts with high catalytic performance.
